category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 07/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect.

questiontext.text: A can do a piece of work in 15 days and B can do it in 20 days. They work together for 5 days and then A leaves. How many more days does B need to finish the remaining work?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 8.75 days

Combined rate = 1/15+1/20 = 7/60 per day. In 5 days: 35/60 = 7/12 done. Remaining = 1−7/12 = 5/12. B alone: (5/12)/(1/20) = 100/12 = 25/3 ≈ 8.33 days.

questiontext.text: The sum of digits of a two-digit number is 9. If the digits are reversed, the new number is 27 more than the original. What is the original number?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 36

Let tens digit = a, units digit = b. a+b=9 and (10b+a)−(10a+b)=27 ⟹ 9(b−a)=27 ⟹ b−a=3. So b=6, a=3. Number = 36.

name.text: P07 Q3 - Profit loss dishonest merchant

questiontext.text: A dishonest dealer claims to sell at cost price but uses a weight of 900 g instead of 1 kg. What is his actual percentage profit?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 11.11%

He sells 900 g but charges for 1000 g. Profit % = (1000−900)/900 × 100 = 100/900 × 100 = 11.11%.

questiontext.text: Knife : Cut :: Needle : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Stitch

A knife is used to cut; a needle is used to stitch.

questiontext.text: The sum of the ages of three persons A, B, C is 90. A is twice as old as B. C is 10 years older than B. What is B's age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 20

A=2B, C=B+10. Sum: 2B+B+B+10=90 ⟹ 4B=80 ⟹ B=20.

questiontext.text: Find the pattern and give the missing term: A2, C4, E6, G8, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) I10

Letters skip one: A, C, E, G, I. Numbers increase by 2: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10. Next: I10.

name.text: P07 Q7 - SI CI difference problem

questiontext.text: The difference between compound interest and simple interest on Rs. 2000 at 5% per annum for 2 years is:
A solid sphere of radius 3 cm is melted and recast into a cylinder of radius 3 cm. What is the height of the cylinder?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 4 cm

Volume of sphere = (4/3)π(3)³ = 36π cm³. Volume of cylinder = π(3)²h = 9πh. So 9πh = 36π ⟹ h = 4 cm.

questiontext.text: From a group of 7 men and 4 women, a committee of 3 men and 2 women is to be formed. How many such committees are possible?
Starting from point X, Rekha walks 4 km South, then 3 km West, then 4 km North. How far is she from point X and in which direction?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 3 km West

South 4 km, then North 4 km cancel. Net: 3 km West from starting point X.

questiontext.text: P's mother is the only daughter of Q's father. How is Q related to P?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Uncle or Mother (sibling of P's mother)

Q's father's only daughter = P's mother. So Q is a sibling of P's mother. If Q is male, Q is P's maternal uncle; if female, P's maternal aunt. Most likely answer: maternal uncle.

questiontext.text: A card is drawn at random from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. What is the probability that it is a king or a red card?

name.text: P07 Q13 - Odd one out instruments

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Violin, Guitar, Sitar, Tabla

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Tabla

Violin, Guitar, Sitar are all stringed instruments. Tabla is a percussion instrument.

questiontext.text: What is the largest prime factor of 420?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 7

420 = 2²×3×5×7. Largest prime factor = 7.

questiontext.text: If A is taller than B, B is taller than C, and C is taller than D, then which statement is definitely true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) A is taller than D

A > B > C > D. By transitivity, A > D. This is definitively true.

answer.text: D is the shortest among all persons.
B is taller than D by the same amount as A is taller than C.
C is shorter than A by more than B.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 07/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect.

name.text: P07 Q16 - Compact operator spectrum zero

questiontext.text: Which of the following statements about \(\limsup\) and \(\liminf\) of a bounded real sequence \((a_n)\) is TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) lim sup aₙ ≥ lim inf aₙ with equality iff the sequence converges

Always lim sup ≥ lim inf. Equality holds iff the sequence converges (to that common value). Both are finite for bounded sequences.

answer.text: \(\limsup a_n\geq\liminf a_n\), with equality if and only if \((a_n)\) converges
\(\limsup a_n=\liminf a_n=0\) for every bounded sequence

questiontext.text: If \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on \([a,b]\) and each \(f_n\) is Riemann integrable, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) f is Riemann integrable and ∫f = lim ∫fₙ

Uniform convergence preserves Riemann integrability and allows interchange of limit and integral: ∫[a,b] lim fₙ = lim ∫[a,b] fₙ.

answer.text: \(f\) is Riemann integrable and \(\int_a^b f=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_a^b f_n\)
the limit and integral cannot be interchanged in general
\(f\) is continuous but not necessarily integrable

name.text: P07 Q18 - Change of variables double integral

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(\iint_D (x^2+y^2)\,dA\) over the disk \(D:x^2+y^2\leq R^2\) using polar coordinates, the integral becomes:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ∫₀²π ∫₀^R r²·r dr dθ = πR⁴/2

x²+y² = r², Jacobian = r. Integral = ∫₀^{2π} ∫₀^R r²·r dr dθ = 2π·[r⁴/4]₀^R = 2π·R⁴/4 = πR⁴/2.

name.text: P07 Q19 - Bounded linear operator norm

questiontext.text: The operator norm of a bounded linear operator \(T:X\to Y\) between normed spaces is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ‖T‖ = sup{‖Tx‖ : ‖x‖ ≤ 1}

Operator norm: ‖T‖ = sup_{‖x‖≤1} ‖Tx‖ = sup_{x≠0} ‖Tx‖/‖x‖. This equals the Lipschitz constant of T.

answer.text: \(\|T\|=\|Te_1\|\) where \(e_1\) is the first basis vector

name.text: P07 Q20 - Diagonalization similar matrices trace

questiontext.text: If \(A\) is a \(3\times 3\) matrix with eigenvalues \(1, 2, 3\), what is \(\text{tr}(A^2)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 14

The eigenvalues of A² are 1²,2²,3² = 1,4,9. tr(A²) = 1+4+9 = 14.

name.text: P07 Q21 - Inner product orthogonal complement

questiontext.text: In a Hilbert space \(H\), if \(M\) is a closed subspace, then every \(x\in H\) can be written uniquely as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) x = m + m⊥ where m ∈ M and m⊥ ∈ M⊥

Orthogonal decomposition theorem: H = M ⊕ M⊥. Every vector decomposes uniquely as the sum of its projection onto M and its projection onto M⊥.

answer.text: \(x\in M\) or \(x\in M^\perp\) but not both

questiontext.text: The degree \([\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2}):\mathbb{Q}]\) of the field extension is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 3

The minimal polynomial of ∛2 over ℚ is x³−2, which is irreducible by Eisenstein (p=2). So [ℚ(∛2):ℚ] = 3.

questiontext.text: The alternating group \(A_5\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) a simple non-abelian group of order 60

A₅ has order 5!/2 = 60. It is simple (no normal subgroups except {e} and A₅ itself), non-abelian, and is the smallest non-abelian simple group.

answer.text: a simple non-abelian group of order 60

questiontext.text: Which of the following implications is TRUE about integral domains?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Euclidean domain ⟹ PID ⟹ UFD

The correct chain: Euclidean domain (ED) ⟹ PID (every ED is a PID) ⟹ UFD. Example: ℤ is ED and PID and UFD. ℤ[x] is UFD but NOT a PID.

questiontext.text: The Schwarz lemma states: if \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) is holomorphic with \(f(0)=0\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) |f(z)| ≤ |z| and |f'(0)| ≤ 1

Schwarz lemma: if f: D→D is holomorphic with f(0)=0, then |f(z)|≤|z| for all z∈D and |f'(0)|≤1. Equality at any interior point or |f'(0)|=1 implies f(z)=e^{iθ}z.

name.text: P07 Q26 - Argument principle zeros poles

questiontext.text: The argument principle states that for a meromorphic function \(f\) inside a simple closed curve \(\gamma\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) (1/2πi)∮_γ f'(z)/f(z)dz = Z − P

Argument principle: (1/2πi)∮ f'/f dz = Z − P where Z = number of zeros and P = number of poles inside γ (counted with multiplicity).

name.text: P07 Q27 - Baire category theorem consequences

questiontext.text: A direct consequence of the Baire category theorem for complete metric spaces is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) a complete metric space cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets

Baire's theorem: a complete metric space is of second category — it cannot be expressed as a countable union of nowhere dense sets. Applications: open mapping theorem, uniform boundedness principle.

answer.text: every complete metric space is compact
a complete metric space cannot be a countable union of nowhere dense sets
every convergent sequence in a complete metric space is bounded
every closed bounded set in a complete metric space is compact

questiontext.text: Duhamel's principle for the inhomogeneous heat equation \(u_t=ku_{xx}+F(x,t)\), \(u(x,0)=0\), gives the solution as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) u(x,t) = ∫₀ᵗ S(t−s)F(·,s)ds

Duhamel's principle: the inhomogeneous solution is u(x,t) = ∫₀ᵗ S(t−s)F(x,s)ds, where S(t) is the semigroup (solution operator for homogeneous equation). This turns a forcing term into a superposition of homogeneous solutions.

answer.text: u is always zero when initial data are zero
\(u(x,t)=\displaystyle\int_0^t S(t-s)F(\cdot,s)\,ds\) where \(S(t)\) is the homogeneous solution semigroup

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet problem for Laplace's equation on the unit disk \(\Delta u=0\), \(u=f\) on \(|z|=1\), has solution given by the Poisson integral formula:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) u(r,θ) = (1/2π)∫₀²π f(φ)·(1−r²)/(1−2r cos(θ−φ)+r²) dφ

Poisson formula for the disk: u(r,θ) = (1/2π)∫₀²π P(r,θ−φ)f(φ)dφ where P = (1−r²)/(1−2r cos(θ−φ)+r²) is the Poisson kernel.

questiontext.text: If \(z=f(x,y)\) satisfies \(F(x,y,z)=0\) implicitly, then \(\partial z/\partial x\) (with \(y\) fixed) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) −F_x/F_z

By implicit differentiation: F_x + F_z·(∂z/∂x) = 0 ⟹ ∂z/∂x = −F_x/F_z (provided F_z ≠ 0).

questiontext.text: Bessel's equation of order \(n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) x²y'' + xy' + (x²−n²)y = 0

Bessel's equation of order n: x²y''+xy'+(x²−n²)y=0. Solutions are Bessel functions Jₙ(x) and Yₙ(x). Important in problems with cylindrical symmetry.

questiontext.text: The Legendre polynomials \(P_n(x)\) satisfy the orthogonality relation on \([-1,1]\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ∫₋₁¹ Pₙ(x)Pₘ(x)dx = 2/(2n+1)·δₙₘ

Legendre polynomial orthogonality: ∫₋₁¹ Pₙ(x)Pₘ(x)dx = 0 if n≠m and = 2/(2n+1) if n=m.

questiontext.text: For \(X\sim\text{Exp}(\lambda)\), the memoryless property states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t)

The exponential distribution is the unique continuous distribution with the memoryless property: P(X>s+t|X>s)=P(X>t) for all s,t>0.

answer.text: \(X\) forgets its past values entirely (distribution changes with each observation)

questiontext.text: If \(\bar{X}\) is the sample mean of an iid normal sample of size \(n\) with unknown variance \(\sigma^2\), the standardized statistic \(T=(\bar{X}-\mu)/(S/\sqrt{n})\) follows:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) t distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom

When σ is unknown and estimated by S (sample standard deviation), T = (X̄−μ)/(S/√n) ~ t(n−1) (Student's t-distribution with n−1 degrees of freedom).

answer.text: Student's \(t\) distribution with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom
chi-squared distribution with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom

name.text: P07 Q35 - Sufficient statistic exponential family

questiontext.text: For the exponential family \(f(x;\theta)=h(x)\exp(\eta(\theta)T(x)-B(\theta))\), the sufficient statistic for \(\theta\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) T(x) (the natural statistic)

By the factorization theorem, for exponential families, T(X) (the natural sufficient statistic) is sufficient for θ. The likelihood factors as g(T(x),θ)·h(x).

questiontext.text: The center \(Z(G)\) of a group \(G\) is defined as \(Z(G)=\{g\in G: gx=xg\text{ for all }x\in G\}\). Which of the following is always TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Z(G) is a normal abelian subgroup of G

Z(G) is always an abelian subgroup (elements commute with everything), and it is normal: for any z∈Z(G) and g∈G, gzg⁻¹ = zgg⁻¹ = z ∈ Z(G).

answer.text: \(Z(G)\) is always a normal abelian subgroup of \(G\)

questiontext.text: The connected subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\) (with the standard topology) are exactly:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) intervals (including rays and the whole line)

A subset of ℝ is connected if and only if it is an interval (possibly degenerate: a single point, a bounded interval, a ray (a,∞), (−∞,b), or all of ℝ). The intermediate value theorem characterizes this.

answer.text: exactly the intervals (bounded or unbounded, open/closed/half-open)

questiontext.text: For the system \(x'=y\), \(y'=-x\), the energy function \(V(x,y)=x^2+y^2\) shows the origin is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) stable (Lyapunov stable) but not asymptotically stable

V = x²+y² > 0 near origin. V̇ = 2xx'+2yy' = 2xy + 2y(−x) = 0. Since V̇=0, V is constant along trajectories (circles). The origin is stable (Lyapunov), but NOT asymptotically stable (trajectories don't approach origin).

answer.text: Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable

name.text: P07 Q39 - Convergence in distribution CLT

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be iid with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2>0\). By the CLT, \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n-\mu)/\sigma\) converges in distribution to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) N(0,1)

Central Limit Theorem: √n(X̄ₙ−μ)/σ →_d N(0,1). The distribution of the standardized mean converges to the standard normal.

questiontext.text: Using residues, \(\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{dx}{1+x^2}\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) π

1/(1+x²) = 1/[(x−i)(x+i)]. Upper half-plane pole at x=i: residue = 1/(2i). Integral = 2πi × 1/(2i) = π.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 07/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4, +2 partial, −1 wrong.

name.text: P07 Q41 MSQ - Banach spaces classical

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Uniform Boundedness Principle (Banach-Steinhaus theorem)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Statement of Banach-Steinhaus. (2) TRUE: Pointwise bounded family of operators is uniformly bounded. (3) TRUE: Application — if Tₙx converges for all x, then {‖Tₙ‖} is bounded. (4) TRUE: Uses Baire category theorem.

answer.text: If \(\{T_n\}\) is a sequence of bounded linear operators on a Banach space \(X\) and \(\sup_n\|T_nx\|<\infty\) for all \(x\), then \(\sup_n\|T_n\|<\infty\).
Pointwise boundedness of a family of operators implies uniform boundedness (on the operator norm).
If \(T_n x\to Tx\) for all \(x\in X\), then \(\sup_n\|T_n\|<\infty\).
The proof of Banach-Steinhaus uses the Baire category theorem.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about ergodic theory?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Ergodic = only trivial invariant sets. (2) TRUE: Birkhoff ergodic theorem. (3) FALSE: Mixing implies ergodic, not the converse. (4) TRUE: von Neumann mean ergodic theorem in Hilbert spaces.

answer.text: A measure-preserving transformation \(T\) is ergodic if every \(T\)-invariant measurable set has measure 0 or 1.
For an ergodic system, Birkhoff's theorem gives time averages equaling space averages almost everywhere.
The mean ergodic theorem (von Neumann) holds in Hilbert spaces: the Cesàro averages of \(T^n f\) converge in \(L^2\).

name.text: P07 Q43 MSQ - Algebraic topology homology

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the homology groups of common topological spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: H_k(S^n)=ℤ for k=0,n and 0 otherwise. (2) TRUE: H₁(T²)=ℤ², H₂(T²)=ℤ. (3) TRUE: H₁(S¹)=ℤ, H_k=0 for k≥2. (4) TRUE: Euler characteristic = alternating sum of Betti numbers.

answer.text: The Euler characteristic \(\chi(X)=\sum_k(-1)^k\,\text{rank}\,H_k(X)\) is a topological invariant.

name.text: P07 Q44 MSQ - Convex functions optimization

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about convex functions \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Definition of convexity. (2) TRUE: Local minimum = global minimum for convex functions. (3) TRUE: Convex functions are continuous on the interior of their domain. (4) TRUE: For smooth f, convex iff Hessian is positive semi-definite.

answer.text: \(f\) is convex if \(f(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)\leq\lambda f(x)+(1-\lambda)f(y)\) for all \(x,y\) and \(\lambda\in[0,1]\).
Every local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum.
A convex function on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is continuous on the interior of its effective domain.
A twice-differentiable function \(f\) is convex iff its Hessian \(\nabla^2 f\) is positive semi-definite everywhere.

name.text: P07 Q45 MSQ - Discrete mathematics graph theory

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about graphs?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Euler's formula V−E+F=2 for connected planar graphs. (2) TRUE: Sum of degrees = 2|E| (handshaking lemma). (3) FALSE: K₅ and K₃,₃ are NOT planar (Kuratowski's theorem). (4) TRUE: A graph is bipartite iff it has no odd cycles.

answer.text: For a connected planar graph, \(V-E+F=2\) (Euler's formula).
The sum of degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges (handshaking lemma).
A graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd-length cycles.

name.text: P07 Q46 MSQ - Hahn Banach theorem

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Hahn-Banach theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Extension form of Hahn-Banach. (2) TRUE: Separation form. (3) TRUE: Every normed space embeds isometrically in its double dual X**. (4) TRUE: A normed space is reflexive iff the natural embedding X→X** is surjective.

answer.text: A bounded linear functional on a subspace of a normed space can be extended to the whole space with the same norm (extension form).
A nonempty convex open set and a disjoint convex set can be separated by a closed hyperplane (separation form).
Every normed space \(X\) embeds isometrically into its double dual \(X^{**}\).
A Banach space \(X\) is reflexive if and only if the natural map \(X\to X^{**}\) is surjective.

name.text: P07 Q47 MSQ - Brownian motion properties

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about standard Brownian motion \((B_t)_{t\geq 0}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Definition of BM. (2) TRUE: Independent increments. (3) TRUE: Almost surely continuous sample paths (Kolmogorov continuity criterion). (4) TRUE: BM is nowhere differentiable a.s.

answer.text: Brownian motion has independent and stationary increments.
Almost all sample paths of Brownian motion are continuous.
Almost all sample paths of Brownian motion are nowhere differentiable.

name.text: P07 Q48 MSQ - Hilbert space operators self-adjoint

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about self-adjoint operators \(A=A^*\) on a Hilbert space?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Real spectrum. (2) TRUE: Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal. (3) TRUE: Spectral theorem. (4) TRUE: ‖A‖ = r(A) for self-adjoint operators.

answer.text: All eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator are real.
Eigenvectors of a self-adjoint operator corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.
Every bounded self-adjoint operator on a Hilbert space admits a spectral decomposition.
For a bounded self-adjoint operator, \(\|A\|=r(A)\) (norm equals spectral radius).

name.text: P07 Q49 MSQ - Algebraic number theory

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about algebraic integers and number fields?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Algebraic integers satisfy monic polynomials with integer coefficients. (2) TRUE: Gaussian integers ℤ[i] are the algebraic integers in ℚ(i). (3) TRUE: Ring of algebraic integers is a ring. (4) FALSE: ℤ[√-5] is not a UFD — (2)(3) = (1+√-5)(1-√-5) and 2,3,1±√-5 are all irreducible.

answer.text: An algebraic integer is a root of a monic polynomial with integer coefficients.
The Gaussian integers \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) are the algebraic integers in \(\mathbb{Q}(i)\) and form a Euclidean domain.
The set of all algebraic integers forms a ring.

name.text: P07 Q50 MSQ - PDE energy methods

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about energy methods for PDEs?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Energy inequality proves uniqueness. (2) TRUE: Energy of wave equation is conserved. (3) TRUE: Heat equation dissipates energy. (4) TRUE: Poincaré inequality is used in energy estimates.

answer.text: Energy methods can prove uniqueness of solutions to the heat and wave equations by showing the energy of the difference of two solutions is zero or decreasing.
The energy \(E(t)=\tfrac{1}{2}\int(u_t^2+c^2 u_x^2)\,dx\) is conserved for the wave equation \(u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}\).
The \(L^2\)-norm of solutions of the heat equation is non-increasing (energy dissipation).
The Poincaré inequality \(\int_\Omega u^2\leq C\int_\Omega|\nabla u|^2\) (for \(u\in H_0^1\)) is used in energy estimates.

name.text: P07 Q51 MSQ - Complex analysis entire functions

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about entire functions (functions holomorphic on all of \(\mathbb{C}\))?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Liouville. (2) TRUE: FTA follows from Liouville. (3) TRUE: Picard's theorem (entire non-polynomial omits at most one value). (4) TRUE: Hadamard factorization for entire functions of finite order.

answer.text: Every bounded entire function is constant (Liouville's theorem).
Every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, follows from Liouville).
A non-constant entire function takes every complex value with at most one exception (Picard's little theorem).
Entire functions of finite order \(\rho\) can be factored using Hadamard's theorem involving their zeros.

name.text: P07 Q52 MSQ - Statistics multivariate normal

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the multivariate normal distribution \(\mathbf{X}\sim N_p(\boldsymbol\mu,\boldsymbol\Sigma)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Linear combinations are normal. (2) TRUE: Uncorrelated components of a multivariate normal are independent. (3) TRUE: Marginal distributions are normal. (4) TRUE: Conditional distributions are also normal.

answer.text: Any linear combination \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{X}\) is normally distributed.
If components of \(\mathbf{X}\) are uncorrelated (off-diagonal entries of \(\boldsymbol\Sigma\) are 0), they are independent.
Marginal distributions of subsets of components are also multivariate normal.
The conditional distribution of some components given others is also normal.

name.text: P07 Q53 MSQ - Lie groups basics

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Lie groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: A Lie group is both a group and a smooth manifold with compatible structure. (2) TRUE: The tangent space at the identity is the Lie algebra. (3) TRUE: The exponential map exp: g → G is a local diffeomorphism. (4) FALSE: Not all Lie groups are abelian; GL(n,ℝ) for n≥2 is non-abelian.

answer.text: A Lie group is a group that is also a smooth manifold, where multiplication and inversion are smooth maps.
The tangent space at the identity element of a Lie group carries the structure of a Lie algebra.
The exponential map \(\exp:\mathfrak{g}\to G\) is a local diffeomorphism near 0.

name.text: P07 Q54 MSQ - Measure theory Radon-Nikodym

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Radon-Nikodym theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Statement: if ν≪μ (ν abs. cont. w.r.t. μ), then ν=f·μ for some f≥0. (2) TRUE: f is the Radon-Nikodym derivative dν/dμ. (3) TRUE: Chain rule for R-N derivatives. (4) TRUE: Used in conditional expectation in probability.

answer.text: If \(\nu\ll\mu\) (absolutely continuous), there exists a measurable \(f\geq 0\) such that \(\nu(A)=\int_A f\,d\mu\) for all measurable \(A\).
The function \(f\) is called the Radon-Nikodym derivative \(d\nu/d\mu\).
The Radon-Nikodym theorem is used to define conditional expectation in probability theory.

name.text: P07 Q55 MSQ - Legendre transform duality

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Legendre-Fenchel transform \(f^*(y)=\sup_x(\langle x,y\rangle-f(x))\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: f* is always convex (as a supremum of affine functions). (2) TRUE: Biconjugate f**=f for convex lsc functions. (3) TRUE: Young's inequality. (4) TRUE: Conjugate of quadratic.

answer.text: The conjugate \(f^*\) is always convex, regardless of whether \(f\) is convex.
For a convex lower semi-continuous \(f\), the biconjugate \((f^*)^*=f\).
Young's inequality: \(\langle x,y\rangle\leq f(x)+f^*(y)\) for all \(x,y\).
